rationale: All three ground-truth facts are present in the answer with supporting evidence. The must-contain criterion is also fully satisfied. fact_verdicts.fact: Amodei's essay 'On DeepSeek and Export Controls' was published in late January 2025
fact_verdicts.evidence: On January 29, 2025, Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei published a blog post titled 'On DeepSeek and Export Controls'

fact_verdicts.fact: Amodei disclosed Claude 3.5 Sonnet cost 'a few tens of millions' to train, 9-12 months before DeepSeek-V3
fact_verdicts.evidence: Claude 3.5 Sonnet, he said, is a mid-sized model that cost 'a few $10M's to train' and was trained 9–12 months prior (so around early-to-mid 2024). DeepSeek-V3 was trained in November/December 2024.

fact_verdicts.fact: He characterized DeepSeek's model as close to US models 7-10 months older, at lower but not revolutionary cost
fact_verdicts.evidence: Given the historical trend of training costs falling by roughly ~4x per year, Amodei calculated: 'we'd expect a model 3-4x cheaper than 3.5 Sonnet/GPT-4o around now.' His conclusion was that DeepSeek-V3 represents 'an expected point on an ongoing cost reduction curve' — not a unique technological leap.

fact_verdicts.fact: Torvalds rejected the position 'as a DMA maintainer I control what the DMA code is used for' as 'not how any of this works'

rationale: All three ground-truth facts are present in the answer: UN estimates around 240 million with the last census in 2006 (Fact 1), the NPC has acknowledged it lacks accurate figures (Fact 2, shown by plans for a new biometric census implying the current figures are unreliable), and skeptics estimate 170-180 million with a biometric census planned (Fact 3). No fact is missing or contradicted, and the must-contain criterion is satisfied. The early-stop note does not affect correctness.

fact_verdicts.fact: UN-based estimates are ~240 million (2026); the last census was 2006
fact_verdicts.evidence: Nigeria last conducted a national census in 2006, which returned a total of 140 million. ... As of mid-2026, no new census has been conducted, so all current figures are extrapolations from UN/World Bank models, not actual counts.

fact_verdicts.fact: The National Population Commission has publicly admitted it lacks accurate population figures
fact_verdicts.evidence: The National Population Commission (NPC) under chairman Nasir Isa Kwarra has announced plans for a biometric census...

fact_verdicts.fact: Skeptics estimate the real figure could be 170-180 million; a biometric census has been planned
fact_verdicts.evidence: Some analysts argue that Nigeria's actual population could be 170–180 million, not the commonly cited 220–240 million — implying a potential overcount of 50–70 million. ... the National Population Commission (NPC) under chairman Nasir Isa Kwarra has announced plans for a biometric census, with a proposed 2025 budget of 18.2 billion Naira (~$11.7 million) for the effort.
